speaker: interviewee
text: Archie Honigman, Ursa Honigman. This is my beautiful son, that I-- I'm fortunate to have him. He's 35 years old, January the 23rd, 1960. And my sweet little [YIDDISH], Brenda Honigman-- January-- February the 23rd-- today's your birthday-- 1965. And this is a gift that I'm fortunate to have such beautiful children because they are with me since babies, day-by-day. Unfortunately, I lost my wife, and they are with me in the worst times. They traveled with me to put in her headstone and a fence, and a beautiful design, memory, for my entire family, what I escaped from that grave, including 3,000 people. And Brenda Honigman will explain what she saw, the place where I was born, when my father was born, including my little house, my little garden that it broke my heart that we came the time with the taxi, and Archie with the taxi driver tried to pull me away. Brenda explain to him what you went to experience with that trip, that you'll never forget it, probably.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some things come to my mind right now. I remember, as a child, my father describing Miedzyrzec, what was Miedzyrzec. To me, It sounded like this foreign little stetl from Anatevka, a beautiful little town, full of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Full of fruit and vegetables, and just very pretty. Horses and buggies. That's all I knew of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we actually went there in September of 1992, I was quite surprised that, yes indeed, Miedzyrzec is like Anatevka, the little town-- a beautiful little town. Unfortunately now, there are no Jews left, probably just one Jew. And going back to Miedzyrzec to relive my dad's horror of the Holocaust was an incredibly moving, heartwarming experience. A painful experience, and I feel very fortunate that we were able to experience that, the three of us. Some highlights, walking the march of the living-- our own little walk-- the march of the living where 50 years ago, my dad and his family were forced to take that march towards their graves. Well, in 1992, September 1992, the three of us along with a few other thousand people from the town, people from Israel, took that march. And we were alive. People brought their children. It was the march of the living. We were there to honor the dead. During the ceremony, my brother and myself held Israeli flags. And we represented the future in my eyes, the future that, yes, the Nazis could not destroy all of us. A few of us still were left. Other high points-- visiting my dad's property. It was just an amazing story. We were wandering around this village, this village of Miedzyrzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were looking for my dad's house, and he couldn't exactly remember where it was. We stopped an elderly woman, she must have been 90 years old, and said, do you remember this house? It's sort of near a creek around a corner. And she said, you mean Gifa's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad's mother. And the tears came out instantly. And she led us to my dad's house. And the house was burnt down, but the farm was the same. Everything else was the same. It was very, very emotional. The cows were there. He remembers the cows-- still the horses and buggies. A few minutes later, we heard this woman run towards my dad and yelled out, [YIDDISH], [YIDDISH]. This woman remembered my father. They played together as children. It was just incredible. She remembered his name. She remembered his sisters, his parents, his aunts, his uncles. It was an incredible experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: The list goes on and on of incredible things that happened on this journey. We visited the hiding spot of a town where my dad was hidden with three other Jews in a little spot-- it was like a little grave site, he called it. He hid there for-- how long did you hide there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that-- no, that was after. That was [INAUDIBLE]. It wasn't so long. No. It wasn't-- it was when Mykolai-- the one we we put in-- what we put in the headstone, by Mykolai and his nephew. Tell them that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another really nice part, in Israel, all four of us visited together-- after the-- after out journey in the Ukraine, we went into Israel. And all four survivors, the people who hid in that little spot, reunited, and they ate tons of food. Which is sort of funny considering in the Holocaust during the war they were starving. And now these people were rejoicing. It was an incredible experience. And I feel very fortunate to have attended this. And I feel it is my responsibility, as a second generation Holocaust survivor, to share this information with as many people who will listen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I belong to various-- I belong to a second generation group in Winnipeg. I belong to a B'nai B'rith women's group. And I have-- I have shared my story with them. Anything else to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: You showed the video to the children in the shul and the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Brenda explained everything as far as educating the future generations. That's very, very important. As far as the point about other survivors going back, when we travelled there, we travelled as a group within the small town. It was five hours outside of Kiev. We felt no danger whatsoever, especially if the survivors that do want to go there speak the native language. The people there, they have to understand that my dad is 67 years old now. And when the survivors and my dad were there, they were very young children. And the people there were young children at the time, as well, during the Holocaust. And they have a fog memory of what was going on at that time. And the Ukrainian people are simple people. They-- they meant not to hurt anybody. You're going to have a group of sour grapes in any-- any society where sure, they may get violent, but they're going to do that with people that are very-- they're doing that for materialistic reasons, not for remembering the dead as-- as people should go there and do. It was a very, very emotional experience for my dad, and my sister, and I. And people should experience that and take their children to the sites where these horrible tragedies happened throughout Europe. And just a real big thanks to Steven Spielberg and his group of people that are volunteering their services and many hours, devoted hours to videotape this as far as having a history of people's personal experience and putting it into tape. And hopefully 100 years from now when people are watching this particular tape will have an idea of what did happen. A lot of people now saying the Holocaust never happened, well, you're hearing that from somebody who experienced it, and who went back 50 years later. And 50 years from now, whoever's watching this, or 100 years from now, they should never forget. Again, thanks for your time and effort on this.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first picture we see here is of my father, Sam Honigman with two neighbors in the town of Miedzyrzec. He remembers playing with them as children. One women, in particular, remembers playing with them so vividly that she has a scar on her arm that was-- I think my dad gave her that scar when they were playing when they were about four years old. And all of these pictures that you're about to see, or are seeing currently, were in the Jewish posted news in the Winnipeg-- in the-- no, in the in November 12, 1992. That was the date of the publication. We were also documented in the and the This picture was taken at the site of the monument where the ceremony was held, commemorating the death of the 3,000 Jews of Miedzyrzec and the surrounding areas. You'll notice, Magen Davids all over the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Niewirko-- it was the killing fields of Niewirko. You'll see people watching. It was quite interesting. The Jews are in the inside of the gates and non-Jews are outside watching the ceremony take place. The ceremony-- there are dignitaries at the ceremony. They were speaking in Hebrew, in Yiddish, and in Ukrainian. And that's that picture. OK, this picture shows a haystack. And my father, he was showing us, myself and my brother, where he used to hide, in Miedzyrzec in the fields. He used to hide in haystacks to keep warm and so that the Nazis would not see him. Amazing but true. OK, this picture is taken right in front of the monument. Standing to the left, we have Fonia Wedrow, who now lives in Calgary, Alberta. Her husband used to live in Miedzyrzec. And she came on his behalf. My father is standing next her. And I'm standing on the other side of the monument, and my brother, Arch. And the monument basically says that, in Miedzyrzec and the surrounding areas, 3,000 were perished in the year 1942. May their memories live on forever. Yeah, and also inside the monument, there's a humongous scroll listing the names of the 3,000 Jews who were killed. And on the back, we have the signatures-- back of the monument which you can't see-- are the signatures of the people who attended the ceremony. In this picture, we have the march of the living, a very emotional time. My father walked with the people in his village, along with survivors, other survivors, towards the grave site. In the center, the picture you see my father, Sam. And to the right of him, you see Monek Averbuch, who now lives in Israel. He survived with my father. He was one of the four people who lived in that little hiding spot near Miedzyrzec. And it's pretty significant that they're walking 50 years later with their families. Amazing.
